content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Robert C. Summers                          Date : Jan. 3, 1977
    Franchise Tax Board
    P.O. Box 1463
    Sacramento, CA  95807


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Kenneth Finney

Subject:

       This memorandum is in response to your memoranda
of June 23, 1976, and August 22, 1976, concerning the reporting requirements imposed by Gov. Code Sections 84210(h)
and 84211.1/  At the outset, let me apologize for the long
delay in providing you with answers to your questions.

       The numbering in this memorandum corresponds to
the numbering in your respective memoranda.  First, with
respect to the June 23 memorandum, you ask several questions
relative to the proper itemization of expenditures on campaign
statements.

       A-1.  Section 84210(h) provides that campaign statements must contain "the full name and street address of each
person to whom an expenditure or expenditures totaling fifty
dollars ($50) or more has been made...."  If the committee in
question is a controlled committee, official committee or
primary purpose committee, the payment to Jones is an expenditure (2 Cal. Adm. Code §18225(b)) and, hence, Jones's full
name and street address must be listed on the committee's
campaign statement.  If, however, the committee is a multi-purpose committee, the payment to Jones would not be an expenditure (2 Cal. Adm. Code §18225(c)); and, thus, his name
and street address would not have to be listed on the committee's campaign statement.

       A-2.  Section 84210(h) also provides that a campaign
statement must contain "the full name and street address of
the person providing the consideration for which any expenditure was made if different from the payee."  Thus, if the
committee in question is a controlled committee, official
committee or primary purpose committee, the name and address
of Acme Office Supply and Dunt's Supplies must appear on the

_________________________
1/ All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

Robert C. Summers
P. 2
Jan. 3, 1977

campaign statement. However, because the payment to Brants
Office Furnishers is less than fifty dollars ($50), it need
not be itemized, even on the campaign statement of one of
the aforementioned types of committees. Moreover, if the
committee in question is a multi-purpose committee, none of
the payments is an expenditure and the provisions of Section
84210(h) are not applicable to the payments.

B-1. Since the payments to both Jones and Brants
are less than the fifty dollars ($50) itemization threshold,
their names and addresses need not be listed on the committee's
campaign statement even if the payments are expenditures.

B-2. Jones's name and address would have to appear
on the committee's campaign statement if the payments were
expenditures (see answers to Question A., supra) since the
cumulative amount of the expenditures made to him would be
more than fifty dollars ($50). However, since the expenditures to Brants fall short of the fifty dollars ($50) threshold, the name and address of Brants would not have to be
listed on the committee's campaign statement.

B-3. The name and address of Brants would have to
appear on the committee's campaign statement(again, assuming
the payments are expenditures) since the cumulative amount
of expenditures made to Brants exceed the fifty dollars ($50)
threshold. Jones's name and address, however, would not have
to be listed.

In your memorandum of August 17, you ask several
questions with respect to cumulation. You also inquire about
the provisions of Section 84206.

1. In determining whether fifty dollars ($50) in
expenditures have been made within the meaning of Section
84210(h), all expenditures since the last postelection statement should be counted. Although this conclusion is not
clear on the face of Section 84210(h), reference to sections
84210(g) and (i) and Section 84211 indicate that this is the
appropriate interpretation. First, to conclude that only
expenditures made during the period covered by the campaign
statement should be counted for the purposes of determining
whether the fifty dollars ($50) threshold has been reached
would mean that the fifty dollars ($50) threshold would be
calculated differently for contributions and expenditures.
Second, Section 84210(i) distinguishes between expenditures
during the period and the cumulative amount (i.e., expenditures since the last postelection statement) of expenditures;
and to apply Section 84210(i) only to committees supporting
or opposing more than one candidate would create the anomalous result of one set of rules relative to the fifty dollars

Robert C. Summers
P. 3
Jan. 3, 1977

($50) threshold for these types of committees and a different
set of rules for committees supporting or opposing only one
candidate. Finally, and most persuasively, Section 84211,
which expressly refers to Section 84210(h), specifies that
expenditures includable in "the cumulative amount" should be
considered when determining whether fifty dollars ($50) has
been expended; and the definition of cumulative amount refers
to expenditures since the last postelection statement. Sec-
tion 82013. Accordingly, although Section 84210(h) is some-
what unclear, all expenditures since the last postelection
statement should be counted in determining whether the fifty
dollars ($50) threshold has been met.

2. Our conclusion with respect to Section 84210(h)
does not affect our previous interpretations concerning cumu-
lative amount for purposes of Section 84210(g). In fact the
interpretation makes Sections 84210(g) and (h) consistent.

3. The answer to this question is yes. See the
answer to question 1., supra.

4. If a candidate has not filed campaign statements
in connection with an election during the first half of the
year, he must file a semi-annual campaign statement no later
than July 31 pursuant to Section 84206. Moreover, this re-
quirement exists whether the candidate will file statements
in connection with an election after July 1. The semi-annual
statement is not, however, a postelection statement for pur-
poses of cumulation.

If you have any further questions, please contact
Rich Eichman or me.

KF:jo

CC: Norman L. Fuller
Ken Waldrum
John Pavalasky

Ted, Rich E., Bob S., Mickey

sections.question_synthetic: The document addresses multiple questions regarding campaign statement reporting requirements under California Government Code Sections 84210(h) and 84211, specifically concerning itemization thresholds, cumulative expenditure calculations, and semi-annual filing requirements for different committee types.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that expenditure itemization thresholds apply cumulatively since the last postelection statement (not just the current reporting period), that multi-purpose committees may have different reporting obligations than controlled/official/primary purpose committees, and that semi-annual statements are required by July 31 but do not reset cumulation periods.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple numbered questions (A-1, A-2, B-1, B-2, B-3, and numbered cumulation questions) with corresponding answers rather than traditional QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections. No verbatim Q/C sections exist as separate headers. Analysis based on question content and answer structure throughout the memorandum. Letter references prior memoranda from June 23, 1976 and August 27, 1976.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The document addresses multiple questions regarding campaign statement reporting requirements under California Government Code Sections 84210(h) and 84211, specifically concerning itemization thresholds, cumulative expenditure calculations, and semi-annual filing requirements for different committee types.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concludes that expenditure itemization thresholds apply cumulatively since the last postelection statement (not just the current reporting period), that multi-purpose committees may have different reporting obligations than controlled/official/primary purpose committees, and that semi-annual statements are required by July 31 but do not reset cumulation periods.

embedding.summary: This 1977 FPPC advice letter responds to inquiries about campaign statement reporting requirements, providing detailed guidance on when payments must be itemized based on the $50 threshold, how to calculate cumulative expenditures, and how different committee types are classified for reporting purposes.
